name: German Expressionism
summary: Studio-built, deliberately unreal sets and extreme light and shadow externalized a character's inner state rather than showing a realistic world.

name: Soviet Montage
summary: Editing itself became the primary storytelling tool: colliding shots generated meaning the individual images did not contain on their own.

name: Film Noir
summary: Low-key lighting, moral ambiguity, and fatalistic narration turned crime and city life into a study of guilt and doubt.

name: Italian Neorealism
summary: Working with real locations, non-professional actors, and everyday hardship after the war, in deliberate contrast to studio polish.

name: French New Wave
summary: Handheld cameras, jump cuts, and direct address broke classical continuity and put the director's presence on screen.

name: New Hollywood
summary: Director-driven films took on morally complex, ambiguous stories and borrowed the location shooting and looser structure of European art cinema.

name: Dogme 95
summary: A shared set of rules stripped filmmaking back to handheld cameras, natural light, and on-location sound to force honesty over spectacle.

name: Streaming Era
summary: Serialized, algorithm-distributed production reshaped budgets, pacing, and release strategy around platforms rather than theaters.
